Direct Response 2.0 (Completed 05/07/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 4
Speaker 1: Quick question. What does direct response even mean? We've all heard the term,
maybe even thought we were using and applying it correctly. But where did it come
from and what does it even mean? So here's a certain definition that you might pull if
you Googled it. Direct response marketing is a type of marketing that elicit a specific
measured response resulting from a consumer's direct response to a marketer. Direct
response marketing facilitates the delivery of a call to action and outcome via direct or
online interaction for immediate feedback and response. Okay, so that's a bit of a
mouthful. Measured specific action. Fair enough and it kind of all made sense for the
time when direct response was born. It was originally coined by this gentleman named
Josiah Wellwood. So he was this Englishman who is credited for sending out the first
catalog, and also being credited as the first person to actually use the term. And to put
that into context, this is 1872, which is about four years after Canada was born and
people were still using carrier pigeons or something like that as a means of
communication.
So while direct response has really remained relevant and effective in producing who
knows how many billions of dollars in the 150 years since its inception, I'm willing to
gamble and bet that the definition is just a little bit too short-sighted and one
dimensional for the tools we have available to us today, as well as the sophistication
level and skepticism of our audiences. So in a world of pixels, cookies, and crazy smart
email automation software, and even crazier and smarter customers I think it's time for
a new definition. I really want to create a new definition, one that's contextually
relevant. We're no longer just sending mailers to people without having any clue where
they're at as a prospect. So can we redefine it to be more contextually relevant, to be
adaptable, to actually be two dimensional? Have that conversation, one that adapts to
the fears, hopes, and objections of your prospect. And then can we actually rephrase it a
little to create the context for a response instead of trying to force one? We now have
the tools to adapt our conversation, to actually create this context, and I think it's really
time we leverage that.
So that's why I am willing to create this new definition. What would direct response 2.0
mean? And where this is coming from is this thesis that your customer's response will
only be as strong as the questions that elicit it. Let me say that again. Your customer's
response will only be as strong as the questions that elicit it, and that is why
collaborative selling or one-on-one selling is so effective and gets 10 times the
conversion rates of typical online marketing methods or direct response. Think about all
the questions that a coach will ask, or a salesperson will ask during that enrollment call.
It's 90% asking questions, hearing the response from the prospect, and adapting the
sales message accordingly. Now, of course that is one-on-one selling, and of course
we're not going to be able to fully replicate that environment. But the closer we can
extract the elements of what makes that effective mostly the questions that are going
on and adapting our messages accordingly, the closer we could come to bridging that
gap, that 10x gap in conversion rates between typical online marketing and one-on-one
enrollment calls.
So that's why it was part of direct response 2.0 as we upgrade the way we perceive
marketing and direct response as a whole,, here is what I want to include within that
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 4 --

Direct Response 2.0 (Completed 05/07/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 4
definition. Direct response 2.0 is intuitive. We're no longer just going for the superficial
two dimensions data, but we're actually piercing levels deeper, asking what it means.
What insights can we, as a marketer, as an intuitive marketer gather from what is being
presented to us, instead of taking it for face value? It's empathy driven. This is a skill
that we all have but don't tap into when it comes to market. We try to press the
persuasion triggers instead of just going a little more subcutaneous and trying to really
tap into what that feeling is. It anticipates the questions customers will have across
every step. One of the practices I do when I write copy is I pretend that I am in a one-on-
one enrollment call with this prospect, and I just kind of have that conversation in my
head, and see what are they asking int his moment. And how can I preempt that? How
can I preempt that and present the answers to the questions in their head before
they've even asked that? As a side benefit, that's what generates those, "Oh, my God.
It's like you read my mind," moments.
Another thing it does is it challenges the prospect's beliefs and self-imposed limitations,
and it makes a new reality feel exciting, believable, and safe. So those are the main
components of direct response 2.0, but it begs the question, even with this new
definition, can we really 10x conversions? I know it's a crazy claim to make and it's not a
claim. It's a thesis. It's a thesis that there is this 10x gap between one-on-one selling and
enrollment calls and digital marketing methods. So while we can't fully replace that one-
on-one dynamic, the goal here really is to extract the major levers, the main ingredients
of what makes that one-on-one dynamic work so well. The things that make people feel
heard, seen, and understood, and do it for 60,000 leads in a single afternoon. And to me
that is a pretty special time when we could do those things at scale. And one of the best
feelings I get as a copywriter or a marketer is when my clients' inboxes get inundated
with replies from clients saying, "It's like you read my mind." I think I had one person
who claimed my third eye was overactive or something like that.
So there's nothing mystical about it. It comes down to being willing to just sit with it
long enough, to really envision being with that customer, preempt those questions, and
make your copy a response to what those questions would be based on those insights
you've gathered and really generated through your own insight. So that's really what I
want to challenge you to do, to ask questions that go beyond the data, beyond what a
customer would even be able to reveal about themselves to themselves, let alone the
person asking. And the pinnacle of this method, what we're really trying to achieve at
the end of the day is to really give customers a sense that we know them better than
they know themselves, that we know what they're going to ask before they even ask it.
And that's why everything I write is question based, and you're going to see how we
apply this direct response 2.0 method in fuller detail in the next module, but everything
I write initially starts with a question I've answered. I do spend some time with the
quantitative data, but then I ask my own questions and I develop my own internal
rendering of it, and that's what really leads to that magnetic copy that just really
generates those, "Oh, my God. This person gets me," moments.
And those are the moments that build trust, and authority, and credibility, and really
play a major part in the conversion. So here's the underlying thing. If the response will
only be as strong as the questions, that elicit it, then we better start asking some better
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 4
questions. And in the next module you're going to get the 47 Golden Questions
workbook. These are really the core questions that I ask and journal on while preparing,
planning, and executing a funnel. Sometimes I will ask extra questions when I'm writing
specific theme plates, and you're going to get those as well. But these are the question
that I will work on myself and with my clients, especially when I'm taking on a new
project and have to get up to speed in a matter of days. And the copywriters in this
program will definitely empathize. You are writing for multiple clients, multiple different
audiences. It's kind of overwhelming and unless you really have this clear and effective
way to quickly onboard yourself to the emotions of that audience, it becomes pretty
difficult. So this is something that I initially created for myself. These are the 47 Golden
Questions that will fuel every phase of your funnel.
And yeah. You won't have to answer them all at once in module two. They are going to
be sprinkled out throughout the course, but you are going to get that workbook and we
are going to be pretty much making it the Bible for this course. Another side benefit to
answering questions, and I really urge you not to skip it, to spend the time to do it
because it really does make writing your copy faster. But the side benefit is you're also
going to gain clarity on your own business and offerings. By answering these questions,
you're going to see if you're really hitting those sweet spots and maybe addressing some
core hidden needs that your audience may be experiencing. I don't exaggerate when I
say that answering this has led to new sales hooks. It's led to new ideas for offerings,
new aspects of your business that address and counter some of those objections and
deep seated beliefs that your audience would be feeling. So I really encourage you to
spend some time with this. Many of the copywriters that I speak with regularly will also
affirm that they spend more time int he research phase, more time than they'd like
cultivating these insights and building these ideas than they do actually executing.
And that's what I want to encourage every product creator, entrepreneur, coach,
consultant, course creator listening to this to do, to give yourself the time to actually sit
and simmer with these questions, to go down those rabbit holes and see what you
uncover about your audience and how your offer connects to it. At the end of the day, it
comes down to this. It comes down to asking why do 97% of perfectly targeted leads not
buy? Most of us are just scrambling around how to get that 2.5% to 2.8%, or that 2% to
3%, or whatever it is. We're looking for those very small incremental bumps, and that's
okay. That will get us further. It will bring more revenue into your business, but at the
end of the day, the big quantum leaps in business don't come from trying to optimize
2% to 3% or 3% to 4%. It comes from asking why do 97% of perfectly targeted leads not
buy? And part of what we're doing here is really not just kind of asking these questions
for our own benefit, but to get the best results, we're actually trying to build up our
prospect's ability to respond.
Build up our prospect's ability to respond. If 97% of people aren't buying, there is a
disconnect. There is a disconnect in our prospect's ability to raise their hand and say,
"Yes. This was exactly what I needed presented exactly in the way I needed to hear it,
and I am going to respond now with my purchase." So what we're doing int his method
is we're also building up our prospect's ability to respond and we're doing that by
empowering them where they've been disempowered. We're getting them present and
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Direct Response 2.0 (Completed 05/07/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 4 of 4
focused with things they've neglected and avoided in the past. We avoid things that are
painful to us. And we as marketers, yes, we try to agitate it, but maybe before we
agitate it, we need to kind of create that space, that distancing, that okayness to even
venture into that place. So these are things we look at. We also help them release
constrictions and fears around their capacity to meet it, so it's not just about amplifying
fear. Sometimes the best thing you could do is actually help reduce it. Tone it down and
enhance their capacity and ability to meet that fear and do something with it.
And we give them those new beliefs, understandings, and inner shifts to move forward,
so it's not a matter of just scaring your prospect into a sale. Fear is a very strong
emotion, but it's also one that keeps people from even looking at something. It leads to
avoidance just as much as it leads to action, so we have to be very mindful about how
we layer that in, and also the counterpunches we do to make it okay to address that
fear. We don't want people scared. We want people empowered to meet the fear, so
there is a difference in that, and that is something we are going to be working with a
little later on. So at the end of the day, I just want to leave you with the idea that the
secret to higher conversions isn't about more persuasion tricks, but getting more
present with who we're talking to and what they need to hear from us in any given
moment to move forward. So the workbook of questions you're going to get is literally
how I get myself super steeped and present with my audience, or at least the audiences
of my clients within hours.
You're going to get that book. You're going to get the walkthrough of the questions in
the next module, but for now, I'm going to take a little break. I'm going to meet you in
the final lesson of module one soon, where you're actually going to get a few more little
mindset shifts that you need to adopt and cultivate to be a successful conversion coach.
I'll see you in the next lesson.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
